text: Ingestion of 30g protein is sufficient to maximize myofibrillar protein synthesis rates during recovery from endurance exercise, with no additional benefit from 45g.
30g protein post-endurance exercise stimulates a ~46% increase in myofibrillar protein synthesis rates compared to carbohydrate alone.

Whole-body net protein balance increases dose-dependently with protein intake from 0g to 45g post-endurance exercise, meaning higher doses still benefit whole-body protein turnover even if muscle protein synthesis is maximized at 30g.
Approximately 70-74% of ingested protein-derived amino acids appear in the circulation after post-exercise consumption, indicating efficient digestion and absorption.

Dietary protein-derived amino acid incorporation into mitochondrial protein increases dose-dependently up to at least 45g protein post-endurance exercise, suggesting higher doses may benefit mitochondrial adaptation.
Post-endurance exercise protein ingestion does not increase overall mitochondrial protein synthesis rates, though dietary protein-derived amino acids are incorporated into new mitochondrial protein.

Co-ingesting protein with 45g carbohydrate post-endurance exercise shifts whole-body net protein balance from negative (~-0.31 μmol/kg/h with 0g protein) to strongly positive (~13.49 μmol/kg/h with 45g protein).
